summary: Gruber argues that Apple will inevitably port iTunes from Carbon to 64-bit Cocoa, not because the transition itself delivers dramatic user-facing improvements, but because Apple systematically moves its platform forward and prunes legacy technology. He counters John Nack's devil's advocate argument that iTunes doesn't need a rewrite by pointing to Apple's pattern of rewriting nearly all system apps to 64-bit Cocoa, including the Finder. He demonstrates concrete user-facing benefits of Cocoa through examples like contextual services and anchored list selection that Carbon apps miss out on. The cross-platform nature of iTunes complicates the transition, but Gruber suggests Apple could follow Adobe Lightroom's architectural model with thin native UI layers, potentially using WebKit as the cross-platform scripting runtime.
key_points: Apple has rewritten nearly all system apps to 64-bit Cocoa not because users demanded it, but because it represents the future of Mac OS X development
New APIs like QuickTime X are 64-bit-only, and since there are no 64-bit Carbon APIs, this effectively means Cocoa-only — creating increasing pressure to migrate
Cocoa apps automatically inherit system-wide improvements like contextual services and anchored list selection, while Carbon apps like iTunes are left behind
iTunes's cross-platform nature is the main complication, but Adobe Lightroom proves a Cocoa Mac version of a cross-platform app is achievable with thin native UI layers
Apple has a strong institutional pattern of pruning legacy technology — Classic, Rosetta, and eventually Carbon — erring on the side of too soon rather than too late
money_quotes: What really matters are features and user experience, not the developer technologies used to make them.
Cocoa was not magic pixie dust that inherently made the Finder radically better.
Apple doesn't just add things to Mac OS X — they remove old things.
Apple worries about the way things should be far more than it worries about continuity with the way things used to be.
The single most remarkable thing about Snow Leopard is that it is smaller than the previous version of the system.
It is an operating system that is — and, going back to its roots at NeXT, has always been — evolving, not just growing. Apple doesn't just add to it. They prune. They churn.
These little bits of functionality and system-wide consistency constitute the Cocoa advantage.
key_insight: Apple's move to Cocoa and 64-bit isn't about immediate user benefits but about systematically pruning legacy technology and preparing for a future where new APIs and capabilities are exclusively available to modern frameworks.
